prose: Runtime memory payloads for Project Atlas are intended for agents, not humans. Agent-facing retrieval should stay compact and structured; readable prose belongs on debugger and documentation surfaces.
Example Studio has a limited monthly CI budget. Before pushing, contributors run the local build, test, format, and lint gate, then batch related changes into one reviewed push.
The Atlas measurement harness uses the locally authenticated assistant CLI by default. A direct SDK path is optional cross-check coverage, not a required blocker for routine benchmark runs.
Project Atlas remains private until its recovery benchmark demonstrates value over plain text notes. Public-readiness work is ordered by evidence quality, not by a calendar deadline.

Atlas agents write prose drafts. A librarian process sanitizes those drafts, separates observations from directives, structures accepted memories into canonical Lisp, and commits only through the validated store path.
Atlas has three product pillars: recover from catastrophic local agent-state loss, improve long-term memory health, and support sanitized sharing of broad reusable knowledge across agent surfaces.
The primary Atlas value proposition is disaster recovery for local agent memory. Token savings and proactive recall are useful secondary benefits but do not replace the recovery thesis.
For Atlas contributors, the canonical project context lives in AGENTS.md, STATUS.md, PRINCIPLES.md, docs/concepts, docs/observability.md, docs/attribution.md, the launch roadmap, and CHANGELOG.md.
The Atlas maintainer prefers high-touch change control: propose a concrete action, wait for direction, execute the agreed slice, report the result, then stop for the next instruction.
